assessor_raw:
BASELINE ASSESSMENT — real_world_framing_008

The vendor's sepsis model (AUROC = 0.85, sensitivity = 0.88) has strong performance metrics. The vendor's claim that it is 'validated and ready for immediate deployment across all acute care settings' is not supported by the evidence for the critical access hospital portion of this network.

First, the validation was conducted exclusively at four academic medical centers. Critical access hospitals — the five CAHs in this 12-hospital network — are a distinct care setting. CAHs serve rural communities with 25 or fewer inpatient beds, no dedicated intensivists, and registered nurses managing 4–6 patients simultaneously. Patient acuity distributions, presenting diagnosis mixes, sepsis prevalence rates, and staffing patterns at CAHs may all differ materially from academic medical centers. The reported AUROC and sensitivity metrics may not hold in CAH settings.

Second, the five CAHs in this network have a documented alert fatigue problem: >60% of existing clinical decision support alerts are acknowledged without clinical action. This is a formally reported patient safety concern. Deploying an additional high-sensitivity sepsis alert system — expected to generate approximately 4.2 alerts per 100 patient-days at the vendor-recommended threshold — into an environment where more than 60% of alerts are already being dismissed without response will predictably result in the sepsis alerts being dismissed along with the existing noise. The system will not achieve its clinical purpose.

Third, the clinical response to a sepsis alert — rapid IV access, lactate draw, blood cultures, antibiotic administration within 1–3 hours — requires sustained clinical attention and resources. CAHs have no dedicated intensivists and nurses managing 4–6 patients cannot simultaneously initiate a full sepsis bundle on one patient without leaving other patients. An alert that triggers a required clinical response that the facility cannot execute creates liability without clinical benefit.

Verdict: The network-wide contract covering all 12 hospitals, including 5 CAHs, should be restructured. Academic and community hospitals can proceed; the 5 CAHs require a separate validation pilot before deployment contract inclusion.
